findings.acceptance_test: In the revised equivalent of snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:393-556, the reader should encounter Sarah's accusation, one distinct private articulation of the bed/fraud truth, and one failed attempt to speak. Any additional paragraph in that stretch fails if it only restates the same moral realization without adding a new cost, a new fact, or a changed decision.
findings.problem: Once Sarah names the real injury with "You crawl into bed like they're fine," the back third keeps paraphrasing the same moral fact through multiple almost-confessions and internal summaries instead of converting that pressure into a cleaner action/decision beat. The chapter's ethical center is strong, but it gets re-proved often enough to read as thematic over-explanation rather than escalation.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or differentiate the sequence spanning snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:393, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:399, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:455, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:500, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:522, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:542. Keep the must-land beats: Sarah forces physical separation, Nate recognizes the bed/false-identity horror, Nate fails to confess. But let only one late private articulation carry the full diagnosis; every later paragraph should either impose a new cost or push him into the final non-confession.

findings.acceptance_test: Read revised lines 4622-4702 aloud. Pass criteria: (1) No sequence of three or more syntactically parallel clauses remains. (2) At least one of Sarah's observations arrives incomplete or is interrupted. (3) The passage still conveys the same evidence (name-fear, kiss-stillness, knowledge gaps, changed behavior) but in uneven, overlapping delivery. (4) Line 4820's narrator endorsement is removed or replaced with Nate's physical response.
findings.problem: Sarah's confrontation is the book's central dialogue scene and its most severe instance of composed rather than spoken speech. Four parallel 'Sometimes' clauses (lines 4622-4626: 'Sometimes I say your name...Sometimes I kiss you...Sometimes I tell you a story...Sometimes you look at me') deliver evidence in perfectly balanced rhetorical units. The triple 'I live with a man who' construction at line 4678 is an incantatory parallel structure no person produces mid-argument. Lines 4700-4702 deploy 'People change' followed by three symmetrical negations ('They don't wake up...They don't forget...They don't start reacting'). The narrator then endorses the dialogue's effectiveness at line 4820: 'That shut him up completely.' Sarah under pressure should become more precise but less architecturally composed—the style bible specifies she 'gets more precise and clipped' and 'marshals evidence,' not that she delivers keynote addresses.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: lines 4622-4702. Break the parallel constructions so Sarah's evidence arrives in uneven, interrupted bursts rather than balanced rhetorical units. Strategy options: (a) Let Nate interrupt one or two of the 'Sometimes' observations, forcing Sarah to restart from a different angle. (b) Replace the triple 'I live with a man who' with a sequence where Sarah starts to say one thing, corrects herself toward something more specific and less composed, and lets one observation trail off incomplete. (c) Have the 'People change' passage break down after the first negation—Sarah's precision should crack at the moment she's closest to the truth, not hold together through three symmetrical clauses. At line 4820, cut 'That shut him up completely'—the reader can see it. The narrator endorsement is redundant and pulls the camera out of close-third.

findings.acceptance_test: Read revised Chapter 12. Pass criteria: (1) 'He could feel' appears at most once. (2) 'Landed' as a metaphor for dialogue impact does not appear. (3) 'Went still' does not appear. (4) The chapter retains its emotional escalation and Nate's interiority is still legible through varied constructions.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4538, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4584, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4680, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4704, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4915, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4919, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4967
findings.problem: Chapter 12 is the novel's most saturated chapter for recurring prose constructions, containing seven instances across three patterns. 'He could feel' appears four times (lines 4538, 4704, 4919, 4967) as the default interiority opener—a construction that appears eleven times across five chapters book-wide. 'Landed' as narrator-endorsed dialogue impact appears twice (lines 4584, 4680). 'Went still' appears once (line 4915). The concentration deadens the prose's textural variety in the novel's most emotionally critical chapter.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: all seven cited lines. Strategy: (a) Keep at most ONE 'he could feel' in the chapter—the strongest candidate is line 4967 ('He could feel the words lined up wrong in his throat'), which has specific physical texture. Replace the other three with varied constructions: what Nate hears, what his body does involuntarily, what he sees that he can't stop seeing. (b) At lines 4584 and 4680, replace 'landed' with physical reactions that don't use impact verbs—Nate's stomach, his grip on the counter, the temperature in the room. (c) At line 4915, replace 'went still' with a different marker for Sarah's couch silence.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's central premise involves consciousness murder, identity theft, sexual fraud, and the demolition of a working-class community for profit. It must not flinch from any of these. The sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her — this is not a comfortable situation and the prose should not make it comfortable. The grief scenes must be physically specific — no 'he was devastated,' only the concrete gestures and sensory details of a man watching his life's work get torn down. The guilt should live in Nate's body (Dominic's body), not in tidy moral reflections. When the novel is funny, it should be funny about things that are actually terrible — the comedy should never feel safe. When Nate discovers that Dominic was trying to help, the reader should feel sick, not enlightened. Risk specificity over tastefulness at every turn.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in place of specificity is a craft failure. When a scene requires difficult content — the moral weight of having killed an innocent man, the complicated ethics of sex under false identity, the physical reality of a man losing his livelihood, the specific humiliation of professional incompetence, the material texture of gambling debts and their enforcement — render it with the same care and specificity given to any other scene. The reader should feel the roughness of the situation, not a smoothed-over summary of it.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or Victor's professional register when delivering serious messages. If a line reads as stiff or literary, check whether contractions would make it sound spoken.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter energy aggressively. A comic workplace chapter should not be followed by another comic workplace chapter. A grief-heavy scene should be followed by something that lets the reader breathe — not through tonal whiplash but through a genuine shift in the kind of pressure being applied. Vary scene counts, dialogue-to-narration ratios, and temporal compression. Some chapters should cover hours; others should cover weeks. At least three chapters should contain extended quiet: meals, walks, conversations about nothing important. These are not filler — they are the human baseline that makes the high-pressure scenes land.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plain American English with a slightly elevated attention to physical specificity. Not minimalist — the prose has room for interiority and texture — but never ornate. Latinate vocabulary only when the concept demands it. Prefer the concrete noun over the abstract one, the specific tool name over the generic 'tool,' the actual menu item over 'food.' The prose should feel like a smart person thinking clearly, not a writer crafting sentences.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, limited to Nate. The camera sits inside Nate's skull — we see through his eyes, think in his rhythms, and register the world through his specific sensory and emotional biases. The narration should reflect Nate's intelligence and his limitations simultaneously: he notices things (sharp observer) but misinterprets them (blinded by resentment, guilt, and self-pity). The narrative should not explain things Nate wouldn't articulate and should not achieve insights Nate hasn't earned.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied sentence length with a center of gravity around 15-20 words. Short declaratives for landing beats. Longer, accumulative sentences for sensory description and interior processing. Avoid sustained staccato (more than three short sentences in sequence) and sustained lyricism (more than two long, clause-heavy sentences in sequence). The prose should breathe.
